requirement_text: This PWS for Strata describes the technical expertise, support, and services required to develop, implement, sustain, and modernize mission essential National Geospatial-Intelligence Agency (NGA) capabilities developed and sustained by the NGA GeoCapabilities Division for two functional areas: Discovery and Dissemination of Geospatial Content and Geospatial Visualization.
This base IDIQ establishes the acquisition framework for delivering the full scope of information technology (IT) capabilities required for each task order issued under this contract.

The primary purpose of the contract is to obtain the required expertise to continue to provide user-facing services that facilitate access to Geospatial Intelligence (GEOINT) data and products in an on-line, on-demand, and geospatially relevant environment to the Intelligence Community (IC), Department of Defense (DoD), and other Federal Government Agencies across the entire National System for Geospatial Intelligence (NSG) and Allied System for Geospatial Intelligence (ASG).

This contract is designed to provide flexibility in satisfying existing and emerging IT requirements required to work within NGA IT infrastructure, complement and support other NGA efforts, and adapt to new and emerging requirements as missions evolve and leading-edge technologies become available.

Provide the technical expertise required to design, develop, maintain, operate, sustain, implement, and improve current and future services and capabilities developed and sustained by the GeoCapabilities Division.
The Contractor must continuously operate, enhance, update, maintain, and sustain current operational capabilities.
The following sections describe the services that must be provided in support of this contract.
Elements of operational baseline management and sustainment must include, but may not be limited to (items are not listed in priority order): - **(U)

Operational Support Tasks:** Tasks required to support operational hardware and software and ensure the operational capabilities are running securely, efficiently and effectively (i.e., system health monitoring, ticket resolution, content/data dissemination, rapid content exposure for crisis response, chat room monitoring and response) - **(U)
Implementation of integrated capabilities in required operational domains utilizing new hardware or software (e.g., COTS, Open Source Software (OSS), and Government off-the-shelf (GOTS)). 2.

Services required to establish, balance, and integrate stakeholder requirements, develop appropriate lifecycle models and process approaches, and analyze alternatives in a manner that optimizes the delivery of capabilities (e.g., optimize performance, reduce infrastructure cost, etc.) while managing risk and cost.

It also includes providing responses as requested to support NGA’s Request for Change (RFC) process, assessing alternatives, and providing estimates of work required to improve existing and/or provide new capabilities, services, and functionality.

Creation and updates to documents must be in line with the NGA Enterprise Systems Engineering Plan (SEP), NSG/ASG Enterprise Test and Evaluation Master Plan (TEMP), and NGA Configuration Management Plan (NCMP).

Additionally, systems and software design must consider and evaluate design tradeoffs and balance technical risk between systems and software, taking full advantage and leveraging NGA ISP/ASP processes and capabilities consistent with SEP and NCMP for the NSG and ASG.
Activities result in secure systems and baselines along with the creation of the associated required security body of evidence (BoE) artifacts.

Examples of BoE artifacts are Plans of Action and Milestones (POAM); and the control implementation and test plans required to meet Assessment and Authorization (A&A) milestone requirements per the following: - (U)
(ISO) must also be aware of and concur with the actions taken by the Contractor team to secure and maintain system security and accreditation and the associated documentation.
In addition to software source code commitment, software delivery includes developing and maintaining all of the required documentation necessary to update, maintain, and test the code.
Delivery consists of the listing and, where appropriate, publishing of all APIs required for ease of provisioning and consumption of other services.
FSRs may be coded as “Emergency Essential.” Emergency Essential personnel will be required to remain on-site in the event of hostilities.

Services required to document the system, thereby enabling stakeholders to understand the underlying technology, configurations, and composition (i.e., OV-1, as-is architecture diagram, list of servers, API documentation, etc.) of the system.
Verification and Validation (V&V) testing must occur throughout the software and system lifecycle.

The Contractor shall use NGA’s enterprise-managed version control (e.g., GitLab) on each of the available security domains as the version control mechanism for all software.
All infrastructure setup shall be stored in the source code version control.
New servers shall have an automated stand-up.
The Contractor shall automatically build, test, and deploy all services, applications, software code, and metadata using NGA’s enterprise, government-managed CI/CD pipeline: 1.
The Contractor shall automate testing such that changes to source code that impact system functionality shall include automated tests that verify the functionality.
Unit, integration, and end-to-end tests shall run automatically when code changes are proposed to the “main” branch in version control.
Code linters and security vulnerability scans shall also run on merge requests to the “main” branch.
If any of these tests or scans fail, merging the changes into the main branch shall not be allowed.
The Contractor shall implement automated accessibility tests for any user-facing functionality.
NGA’s enterprise, government-managed CI/CD pipeline shall be used for running tests and builds as well as scans for code quality and vulnerabilities. 2.
The Contractor shall automate deployments using NGA’s government-managed CI/CD pipeline.
New versions of a product shall be automatically deployed to non-production and production environments.
Automated production deployment of a new version shall be scripted to happen without any downtime, and teams shall be able to roll back any change quickly.
Deployments shall be implemented to eliminate the need for scheduled maintenance and reduce the coordination between systems for production releases.
The Contractor shall document, register, share, and maintain all APIs, associated data, and documentation in NGA’s enterprise-managed Developer Portal on all domains where the APIs are available.

APIs shall be documented in industry-standard API-specification formats (e.g., OpenAPI, RESTful API Modeling System (RAML)), rather than Portable Document Formats (PDFs) or Word documents, and they shall be accessible via self-service in a development environment with a standardized path to production.
API documentation shall be readily available to all developers at NGA.
APIs shall follow GEOINT standards and be able to be automatically tested for standards compliance.
The Contractor shall be responsible for ASP requirements, including integrating and coordinating all necessary activities with the ISP.
Such coordination shall include, but is not limited to: 1.
The Contractor shall provide an overarching contract Program Manager (PM) to ensure total program integration and support during all phases and across contract task orders.
The PM shall provide program health status for cost, schedule, and performance.

The PM shall manage and administer the programmatic and technical efforts associated with this PWS to include, but may not be limited to, contract administration, cost, schedule, security administration, configuration control, and property control.

The Contractor PM shall attend requested meetings and technical exchange meetings to discuss a myriad of topics that may include, but may not be limited to, staffing, resource allocation, cost, schedule, performance, capability integration and/or interoperability, workflow and/or process optimization, etc.
The outgoing Contractor shall conduct a seamless transition of the capabilities and services to the follow-on Contractor (if applicable).

Both the incoming and outgoing Contractors shall develop and maintain a Transition Plan that describes the process, approach, and schedule for providing an orderly transition of duties, activities, and functions of tasks, tools, equipment, and information knowledge transfers during the contract’s transition terms.
The incoming Contractor shall lead a contract kickoff meeting within ten business days after the contract award.
At a minimum, the incoming Contractor shall introduce key personnel, team members and staff, and discuss organizational structure and administrative operations pertinent to this requirement.

To minimize any decreases in productivity and to prevent possible negative impact on additional services, the incoming Contractor shall have all key personnel on board during the phase-in period commencing with the date of contract award.

As part of the proposal, the incoming Contractor shall provide a draft Phase-In Transition Plan that addresses assumptions, proposed schedule, checklists, and known risk and a final plan 15 business days after contract award.

The incoming Contractor shall address details of overlapping with the outgoing Contractor to include hand-off of any source code, cloud and infrastructure access, configurations, instructions, Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs), databases, and transfer of all GFE, Government Furnished Information (GFI), and Property (GFP).

Key activities that shall occur during the phase-in transition period include (but may not be limited to): becoming familiar with performance requirements, obtaining IC badges, obtaining Common Access Cards (CAC), submitting HSPD-12 documents, completing Government required training, transferring of all source code, documentation, and SOPs developed and maintained by the incumbent Contractor, conducting inventories and transferring all GFE/GFI/GFP.
Phase-Out activities must facilitate and allow for an orderly turnover of information, facilities, equipment, and records and help ensure service continuity.

The outgoing Contractor is ultimately responsible for performing full services IAW the contract during the phase-out period and shall not defer any requirements to avoid responsibility or transfer such responsibility to the incoming Contractor.
The outgoing Contractor shall fully cooperate with the incoming Contractor and the Government, to avoid interfering with the mission, work, or duties.
The Phase-Out Transition Plan shall be submitted 90 days after the last option period is exercised.
Execution of the Phase-Out Transition Plan shall begin no later than 60 days before the contract end date.
At a minimum, the Phase-Out Transition Plan and activities shall address the following: 1.
Procedures to ensure that services and performance objectives required by the contract are met throughout the end of the contract. 2.

All GFI released to the outgoing Contractor or created in the performance of this contract shall be returned to NGA upon termination of the contract; or when no longer required in the performance of the contract as directed by the Contracting Officer (CO).
The Contractor shall provide fully qualified and cleared personnel to satisfy the conditions of this PWS.

The Contractor shall maintain a stable workforce while minimizing turnover, maintaining the same level of expertise and managing their personnel in such a way as to minimize the impact of any turnover and/or disruption to the customer and/or mission.
The Contractor shall develop and maintain a current staffing list.
The staffing list shall contain the names, job titles, employers, and contact information of all Contractor and Subcontractor personnel assigned to the contract.
The staffing list shall be updated and provided as part of the PMR (CDRL A0011).

It shall identify any personnel that left the contract during the previous month, personnel scheduled to onboard during the current or following month, and any personnel scheduled to depart, including the planned departure date.

The Contractor is responsible for ensuring all personnel performing work under this contract possess and maintain current certifications as required within the PWS during the execution of this contract.
Contractor employees shall stay compliant with all NGA mandatory training.
The Contractor shall ensure all Contractor personnel comply with all local, state, and federal ordinances, statutes, and regulations regarding personal safety in work areas.
All key personnel must have an active Top Secret/Sensitive Compartmented Information (TS/SCI) with Counter-Intelligence (CI) polygraph at contract start.
Must be the single point of contact for any programmatic issues that may arise.
The Contractor must also designate an individual to be the backup for the PM to ensure the Government has uninterrupted access to the Contractor.

Once the Contractor knows a key personnel employee is leaving and will no longer support the program, the Contractor shall provide written documentation to the Contracting Officer’s Representative (COR) within one (1) business day of the employee advising the Contractor of their intent to leave.
In the case of a no-notice departure, the Contractor shall immediately inform the COR.

This written notification shall include the date and time the position will be vacant, the anticipated replacement date, and management action, if needed, to ensure tasks and mission remains on schedule toward completion.
No key position shall remain unfilled for greater than two (2) consecutive months.
The Contractor shall notify the Government before making any changes to key personnel.

Before directing any of the actual or proposed key personnel to other programs or functional areas, the Contractor must submit justification to substitute key personnel at least 30 days prior to a substitution.
The Contractor must provide the replacement’s resume to permit evaluation of the impact of the proposed change on the program and its schedule.
The Contractor must not substitute or change the key personnel without the prior written consent of the CO.
The Contractor must demonstrate to the satisfaction of the CO that the qualifications for the prospective personnel are equal to or better than the qualifications of the personnel being replaced.
The Contractor shall update the COR concerning changes to non-key personnel assigned to this contract within three (3) business days of departure.
This relationship shall be achieved through an effective QCP dedicated to ensuring the best possible products and services to end users.
The Contractor shall provide a written QCP IAW this PWS as outlined in the deliverables schedule.

The Contractor shall establish and maintain a QCP to ensure the contract requirements are provided as specified and ensure performance effectively achieves specific quality requirements addressed in the PRS of each task order and IAW the QASP.
The QCP shall describe the inspection system for the requested services.
The Contractor QCP shall be finalized IAW the discussions and submitted not more than 35 days after contract award.
The QCP shall be updated as changes occur.
Each update shall be submitted to the COR for review and acceptance.
The Contractor’s QCP shall demonstrate its prevention-based outlook by meeting the objectives stated in the PWS throughout all areas of performance.
The QCP shall be developed to specify the Contractor’s responsibility for management and quality control actions to meet the terms of the contract.
The QCP shall, at a minimum, address: 1.

The Contractor shall reply in writing within five (5) business days from the date of receipt of the CDR, giving the reasons for the unsatisfactory performance, corrective action taken, and procedures to preclude recurrence.

If the Contractor receives a CDR, the Contractor shall develop and deliver a Corrective Action Plan in the manner and within the time frame specified by the Government, specifying how and by what date it shall bring performance up to the required levels.
The Contractor shall develop and maintain a PmP for effective program management.

The PmP shall describe the management and oversight of all activities performed by the Contractor personnel, including subcontractors, to satisfy the requirements identified in this PWS and all task orders.

The PmP shall describe the proposed management approach, including the management structure, process narratives, schedule/milestone management, reoccurring meetings, tasks and subtasks, a Risk Management Plan/Process, a Configuration Management Plan, Communications Plan, a Security/Continuous Monitoring Plan, a Contract Work Breakdown Structure (CWBS), and a Subcontractor Management Plan.
Recurring meetings shall be documented with minutes, action items, risk items, and other required documentation.
The PmP shall include associated responsibilities and partnerships with other organizations.
The Contractor’s PmP shall be maintained and updated to reflect changes during program execution, subject to Government approval.
The Contractor shall provide an IMS.
The IMS shall include activities/milestones across all task orders.
It also describes compliance with the policies, procedures, and standards required to satisfy the requirements identified in this PWS.
The Contractor shall include approaches in its ADMP demonstrating continuous improvement throughout the system lifecycle.
The ADMP shall be updated annually after the contract award.
At a minimum, the ADMP shall address the following: 1.

The Contractor shall conduct a monthly PMR with the CO, COR and other Government stakeholder personnel to discuss and review the Contractor's performance, schedule, financials, staffing, risk, deliverables, and CDRL status relevant to the reporting period for all task orders.

Risks to successful, timely completion of the contracted effort, including the likelihood of occurrence, impact on overall schedule, performance, and recommended/planned risk mitigation, shall be identified.
Potential opportunities shall also be highlighted.
The Contractor shall notify the Government of problems, issues, concerns, or needed support, if any, being experienced.
Appropriate action shall be taken to resolve outstanding Contractor performance issues identified by the Government.
These meetings shall be at no additional cost to the Government.
The contractor shall submit a Payment Report specific to CLIN(s)
The report shall be provided using Microsoft Excel and contain two tabs: 1)
The Contractor shall provide a WAR identifying accomplishments, issues, roadblocks, engagements, upcoming activities, and other items of interest for all task orders.
The Contractor shall highlight all impediments that require Government assistance during the WAR.
The WAR shall minimally provide the following: 1.
The Contractor shall attend any post-award conference convened by the contracting activity or contract administration office IAW Federal Acquisition Regulation (FAR)

Upon expiration of this contract, the Contractor shall provide the Government with a CCR which, at a minimum, shall include all up-to-date critical information needed to sustain operations, the final status of all capabilities, location and inventory of files and documentation, the status of contract funds and final invoice, disposition of GFE/GFP, the names of personnel who worked on the contract, and written confirmation that they have been read off the program IAW NGA security policy.
The Contractor shall submit a CCR at the end of the last option year IAW the timeline directed by the Government.

At contract award, the Contractor shall have an accredited Secure Compartmented Information Facility (SCIF) capable of accommodating the proposed number of personnel with desks, computers, monitors, and secure phones.
The SCIF shall contain (at a minimum) the following active networks: Joint Worldwide Intelligence Communications System (JWICS), SECNet and SBU.
The secondary place of performance shall include NGA designated facilities such as NGA WASHINGTON Springfield, NGA ST LOUIS St.
Telework (ad hoc) may be allowable but shall not commence until it has been approved by the CO and COR, with a copy of the approved agreement provided to the COR.
Regardless of telework allowability, the Contractor shall be responsible for performing all classified requirements of the contract.

All Contractor employees and/or subcontractor employees identified for telecommuting must complete the annual Computer Security Awareness Training requirement and submit a request for telecommuting through the designated COR.

Contractor and/or subcontractor employees shall not telecommute on Federal holidays, other non-work days, or in the case of a pandemic or other emergency or unforeseen situation such as an epidemic, natural disaster, early closing or delayed opening of the Government, as well as a Government shutdown without the prior written approval of the CO and COR.
The Contractor shall maintain adherence to proper Security classification rules and standards while operating in a telework environment.
Oversight of telecommuting employees shall be IAW contractual requirements and company policy, which the Contractor will manage.
Company telecommuting policies shall ensure that telecommuting worksites do not include public locations (such as libraries, food establishments, parks, or other public locations).
All travel requires advanced approval in writing by the COR prior to the requested travel date.
The Contractor shall provide a written travel request to the COR ten days prior or as soon as possible to the requested travel date.
The Contractor shall maintain an annual travel plan and provide updates quarterly.
The Contractor shall submit monthly invoices for reimbursement citing the appropriate Contract Line Item Number (CLIN)
Each individual travel instance shall be captured.
Copies of receipts, travel vouchers, etc., shall be attached to the invoice to support charges.
Original receipts shall be maintained by the Contractor and made available to Government auditors upon request.
Contractor travel shall be IAW Government-approved lodging and per-diem rates.

All Contractor ODCs shall be approved by the Government CO or COR before the Contractor incurs any expenses; this includes any commercial software licenses to be used, which shall be submitted to the CO or the COR for NGA approval prior to purchase and installation.
Any commercial software license purchased under this contract shall be licensed to the Government and shall be registered in the name of the Government.
The Contractor shall maintain, apply, and perform DoD IC Supply Chain Risk Management (SCRM) activities for CONUS and OCONUS material purchases and shipping.
